solution: 
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <cmath>
#include <unordered_set>
#include <queue>
#include <limits>
#include <functional>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    double minTime(int n, int k, int m, vector<int>& time, vector<double>& mul) {
        if (n > k && n % (k - 1) != 1) return -1.0;

        sort(time.begin(), time.end(), greater<int>());
        int totalPeople = time.size();
        vector<int> left(time.begin(), time.end());
        vector<int> right;
        double totalTime = 0.0;
        int stage = 0;

        while (left.size() > 0) {
            // Select top k people from left
            vector<int> group(left.begin(), left.begin() + min(k, static_cast<int>(left.size())));
            left.erase(left.begin(), left.begin() + min(k, static_cast<int>(left.size())));
            right.insert(right.end(), group.begin(), group.end());

            // Time to cross
            double crossTime = group[0] * mul[stage];
            totalTime += crossTime;
            int steps = floor(crossTime) % m;
            stage = (stage + steps) % m;

            if (left.empty()) break;

            // Select fastest person to return
            int fastestInRight = right[0];
            for (int i = 1; i < right.size(); ++i) {
                if (right[i] < fastestInRight) {
                    fastestInRight = right[i];
                }
            }

            // Remove the fastest from right and add back to left
            auto it = find(right.begin(), right.end(), fastestInRight);
            right.erase(it);
            left.push_back(fastestInRight);

            // Time to return
            double returnTime = fastestInRight * mul[stage];
            totalTime += returnTime;
            steps = floor(returnTime) % m;
            stage = (stage + steps) % m;
        }

        return totalTime;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n^2)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a greedy approach to transport the strongest rowers first and always sends the fastest available person back with the boat. This minimizes the return time. The algorithm sorts the rowers by strength and iteratively transports groups of up to k people. It handles the cyclic stage transitions and calculates the total time by considering both crossing and return trips. The time complexity is O(n^2) due to the nested operations for finding the fastest returner. The space complexity is O(n) for storing the left and right groups. The greedy strategy is efficient but may not always find the absolute optimal solution in complex scenarios.
